sentences.base_language: Rania and Tariq went out for dinner to celebrate her promotion.
sentences.target_language: خرجت رانيا وطارق لتناول العشاء احتفالاً بترقيتها.
sentences.romanized: kharajat Rania wa-Tariq li-tanawul al-asha' ihtifalan bi-tarqiyatiha.
sentences.literal: Went-out Rania and-Tariq for-eating the-dinner celebrating-of her-promotion.
sentences.grammarNote: خَرَجَت (kharajat, went out — feminine verb) agrees with رَانِيَا (Rania, feminine) as the first-listed subject. In VSO sentences, the verb agrees with the nearest subject in gender only when it precedes multiple subjects. لِتَنَاوُل (li-tanāwul, for eating) — verbal noun Form VI of ن-و-ل (to reach/receive): تَنَاوَلَ = to take/consume food. اِحتِفَالاً بـ (iḥtifālan bi-, celebrating) — verbal noun Form VIII of ح-ف-ل (to celebrate), used as an adverbial of purpose.

ألف مبروك (alf mabruk) literally means 'a thousand blessings' and is the standard Arabic expression of congratulations. مبروك alone also works. The phrase is used for promotions, marriages, new homes, and births. Response: الله يبارك فيك (Allah yubarak fik) — 'may God bless you.'

sentences.base_language: Thank you! I'm so happy. Let's order.
sentences.grammarNote: سَعِيدَة (saʿīda, happy — feminine) agrees with رَانِيَا (Rania, feminine). Arabic adjectives always agree with the noun they describe in gender: سَعِيد (masculine), سَعِيدَة (feminine). لِنَطلُب (li-naṭlub, let's order) — the cohortative لِـ + first-person plural verb: a standard way to say 'let's do X.' From root ط-ل-ب (ṭ-l-b, to request/seek).

sentences.base_language: The waiter brought the menu and described the specials.
sentences.target_language: أحضر النادل قائمة الطعام وشرح أطباق اليوم.
sentences.romanized: ahdara an-nadil qa'imat at-ta'am wa-sharaha atbaq al-yawm.
sentences.literal: Brought the-waiter list the-food and-explained dishes the-day.
sentences.grammarNote: أَحضَرَ (aḥḍara, brought/produced) — Form IV of ح-ض-ر (ḥ-ḍ-r, to be present): أَحضَرَ = to bring/present (causative: to make present). قَائِمَة الطَّعَام (qāʾimat aṭ-ṭaʿām, menu) — iḍāfa: قَائِمَة (list/menu) + الطَّعَام (the food). أَطبَاق اليَوم (aṭbāq al-yawm, dishes of the day/specials) — أَطبَاق is the broken plural of طَبَق (plate/dish), pattern أَفعَال.

sentences.base_language: We would like to start with a mezze platter, please.
sentences.target_language: نريد أن نبدأ بطبق مزة، من فضلك.
sentences.romanized: nurid an nabda' bi-tabaq mazza, min fadlak.
sentences.literal: We-want that we-begin with-plate mezze, from your-favor.
sentences.grammarNote: نريد أن نبدأ (nurid an nabda') = 'we want to begin.' The construction verb + أن + subjunctive is the standard Arabic way to express wanting to do something. أن (an) introduces the subjunctive verb. Compare: أريد أن أذهب (I want to go), يريد أن يأكل (he wants to eat).

sentences.base_language: And for the main course, the grilled fish looks wonderful.
sentences.target_language: وللطبق الرئيسي، السمك المشوي يبدو رائعاً.
sentences.romanized: wa-lil-tabaq ar-ra'isi, as-samak al-mashwi yabdu ra'i'an.
sentences.literal: And-for-the-dish the-main, the-fish the-grilled appears wonderful.
sentences.grammarNote: سمك (samak) is a collective noun in Arabic meaning 'fish' as a category or substance. To refer to one fish, you add ة: سمكة (samaka). This collective-to-singular pattern applies to many natural things: شجر (trees/wood) → شجرة (a tree), خبز (bread) → خبزة (a loaf). The collective has no plural in the usual sense — it refers to the whole category.

sentences.base_language: Excellent choice. The fish is very fresh today.
sentences.target_language: اختيار ممتاز. السمك طازج جداً اليوم.
sentences.romanized: ikhtiyar mumtaz. as-samak tazij jiddan al-yawm.
sentences.literal: Choice excellent. The-fish fresh very today.
sentences.grammarNote: اِختِيَار مُمتَاز (ikhtiyār mumtāz, excellent choice) — a nominal exclamation with no verb. اِختِيَار is the Form VIII verbal noun of خ-ي-ر (kh-y-r, to choose/be good): اِختَارَ = to choose. مُمتَاز (mumtāz, excellent) — Form VIII active participle of م-ي-ز (m-y-z, to distinguish): اِمتَازَ = to be distinguished/excel. السَّمَك طَازِج (as-samak ṭāzij) — definite subject + indefinite predicate: a standard nominal sentence structure.

sentences.base_language: They also ordered a green salad and some warm bread.
sentences.target_language: طلبا أيضاً سلطة خضراء وبعض الخبز الدافئ.
sentences.romanized: talaba aydan salata khudra' wa-ba'd al-khubz ad-dafi'.
sentences.literal: They-ordered also salad green and-some the-bread the-warm.
sentences.grammarNote: طَلَبَا (ṭalabā, they two ordered) — the dual past-tense verb form, ending in ـَا (-ā). Arabic has a dedicated dual conjugation for exactly two subjects. Compare: طَلَبَ (he ordered), طَلَبَا (they two ordered), طَلَبُوا (they [plural] ordered). سَلطَة خَضرَاء (salṭa khaḍrāʾ, green salad) — خَضرَاء is the feminine of أَخضَر (green), the elative/colour pattern أَفعَل/فَعلَاء.

sentences.base_language: The food arrived and everything looked beautiful.
sentences.target_language: وصل الطعام وبدا كل شيء جميلاً.
sentences.romanized: wasala at-ta'am wa-bada kull shay' jamilan.
sentences.literal: Arrived the-food and-appeared all thing beautiful.
sentences.grammarNote: وَصَلَ الطَّعَام (waṣala aṭ-ṭaʿām, the food arrived) — VSO order: verb first, then subject. بَدَا (badā, appeared/seemed) — Form I of ب-د-و (b-d-w, to appear): defective verb ending in و, which becomes ا in past tense. كُلُّ شَيء (kullu shayʾ, everything) — كُلّ (all/every) + indefinite noun. جَمِيلاً (jamīlan, beautiful — accusative) is the predicate of بَدَا.

sentences.base_language: For dessert, they shared a plate of kunafa.
sentences.grammarNote: تَشَارَكَا (tashārakā, they two shared) — Form VI dual past: the ـَا (-ā) suffix marks the dual. Form VI تَفَاعَلَ of ش-ر-ك (sh-r-k, to share/partner) implies mutual action: تَشَارَكَ = to share with each other. كَحَلوَى (ka-ḥalwā, as dessert) — the prefix كَ (like/as) + حَلوَى (sweets/dessert, a defective noun from root ح-ل-و, to be sweet).

sentences.base_language: Shall we toast? To your success!
sentences.grammarNote: نَرفَع نَخباً (narfaʿ nakhban, shall we toast?) — نَرفَع from root ر-ف-ع (r-f-ʿ, to raise/lift): Form I. نَخباً (nakhban, a toast) is an indefinite accusative (tanwin ـاً) direct object. لِنَجَاحِكِ (li-najāḥiki, to your success — feminine) — لـ (to/for) + نَجَاح (success, verbal noun of نَجَحَ, to succeed) + possessive suffix كِ (your, feminine).

sentences.base_language: They raised their glasses of juice and smiled.
sentences.grammarNote: كُوبَي العَصِير (kūbay al-ʿaṣīr, the two glasses of juice) — كُوبَي is the dual genitive/accusative of كُوب (glass/cup): the dual suffix ـَي (-ay) replaces the nominative ـَان (-ān) after a preposition or in iḍāfa. اِبتَسَمَا (ibtasamā, they two smiled) — Form VIII dual past of ب-س-م (b-s-m, to smile): اِبتَسَمَ = to smile.
